g.data-narration: An array in Java is a container that holds a fixed number of values of a single type, making data organization simple.
g.text: An array is a container object that holds a
fixed number of values of a

g.data-narration: Arrays are the foundation of all data structures. They appear frequently in exams and real-world applications like image processing and databases.
g.text: Why Study Arrays? - Foundation of Data Structures

g.data-narration: Java arrays have five key properties: fixed size, homogeneous elements, zero-based indexing, they are objects, and stored in contiguous memory.
There are two syntaxes for declaring arrays in Java: int[] numbers (preferred) or int numbers[]. Both create a reference to an array.
Arrays can be initialized in three ways: separately, all at once with curly braces, or using anonymous arrays passed directly to methods.
Single-dimensional arrays store elements in a linear sequence. They can hold primitives like int, double, or objects like String.
Two-dimensional arrays are matrices with rows and columns. Use matrix[row][column] to access elements, like table[1][2] accessing row 1, column 2.
Jagged arrays are arrays of arrays where each row can have different lengths. This creates triangular or irregular data structures.
Use traditional for loops when you need the index, or enhanced for-loops for simple read-only traversal without index modification.
g.text: Use when: need index, modify elements
Use when: read-only, no index needed

g.data-narration: Key array operations include finding length with .length property, copying using clone or System.arraycopy, and sorting with Arrays.sort method.
The Arrays class in java.util provides static utility methods: toString for printing, fill for initialization, and equals for comparison.
Arrays can hold objects including Strings. Each element is initialized to null until assigned. Access object properties using dot notation.
Two common errors: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException when accessing invalid indices, and NullPointerException when using uninitialized arrays.
Arrays have fixed size and can hold primitives; Collections are dynamic, can only hold objects, and provide richer methods like add and remove.
g.text: Fixed size • Primitives OK • Faster • Basic ops
Dynamic size • Objects only • Rich API

g.data-narration: Remember: arrays have zero-based indexing, fixed size after creation, use .length property (not method), and can hold both primitives and objects.
